content:
ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration Yuxi Ma a , Yue You b, Lifeng Wang a, Guoliang Yang a, Si Qin b, Yuyu Su c, Mandeep Singh b, Jianwei Lu a, Dan Liu a,*, Weiwei Lei a,** a Department of Applied Chemistry and Environmental Science, School of Science, RMIT University, Melbourne, Victoria, 3000, Australiab Institute for Frontier Materials, Deakin University, Locked Bag 20000, Geelong, Victoria, 3220, Australiac Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, School of Engineering, RMIT University, Melbourne, Victoria, 3000, Australia

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:Aramid nanofiberBoron nitride nanosheetsThin-film composite membraneOrganic solvent nanofiltration

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: A B S T R A C T Two-dimensional (2D) nanomaterial-based membranes, such as hexagonal boron nitride nanosheets (BNNS), offer exceptional permeance for organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) but suffer from poor mechanical stability under pressure. To address this, we integrated aramid nanofibers (ANFs) with ball-milled BNNS to construct mixed-dimensional thin-film composite membranes (ABN) with enhanced structural robustness and separation performance. The ABN membranes exhibit a tunable thickness (~1.17 μm), surface roughness (~82 nm), and contact angle (~81◦). A representative ABN membrane achieved ethanol flux of 104 L m-2 h-1 at 1 bar, increasing to 164 L m-2 h-1 at 4 bar, with Evans blue rejection slightly decreasing from 96 % to 92 %. The membranes also showed high permeance in methanol and acetone, reversible deformation under pressure up to 10 bar, and stable performance over 24 h in ethanol and under mildly acidic conditions. This study presents a mechanically reinforced, high-performance OSN membrane platform that overcomes the pressure fragility of conventional 2D systems, offering strong potential for practical solvent separation applications.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: 1. Introduction Nanofiltration technology has been widely recognized for its po­tential to reduce costs associated with converting wastewater into valuable resources, as well as processing organic solvents from the pharmaceutical and chemical industries [1]. Organic solvent nano­filtration (OSN) offers significant advantages over traditional separation and purification methods, particularly in the removal of size-specific contaminants and the recovery of organic solvents used in chemical synthesis [2]. However, achieving a comprehensive balance between the permeance-selectivity trade-off and the contemporary requirements for maintaining high mechanical strength and resistance to harsh condi­tions, such as high-polarity organic solvents and acidic or alkaline en­vironments, remains challenging [3]. Numerous efforts have been made to explore potential candidates for organic solvent nanofiltration, particularly focusing on fibrous or poly­meric thin-film composite (TFC) membranes [4–6]. They are advanced in many aspects, such as relatively low cost and ease of fabrication and scalability. However, most polymeric materials and membranes suffer from chemical and thermal instability, with some even dissolving when exposed to harsh organic solvents for extended periods [7].

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: and membranes suffer from chemical and thermal instability, with some even dissolving when exposed to harsh organic solvents for extended periods [7]. Aramid nanofibers (ANFs), known for their robust mechanical strength and high thermal and chemical stability, are promising candidates to meet the stringent stability requirements necessary for a wide range of potential organic solvents used in the OSN process [8,9]. Nevertheless, the flux of ANF based polymeric membranes still falls short of meeting the practical production efficiency requirements. Through adjusting the degree of deprotonation of ANFs, it has been proven that the permeance of ANF dense membrane can be further increased [4,10]. However, the current flux of ANF-based membranes is still insufficient to meet the demands of large-scale production. Two-dimensional (2D) material-based membranes, particularly those based on graphene oxide (GO), hexagonal boron nitride nano­sheets (BNNS), and transition metal dichalcogenides, have garnered increasing attention due to their high permeance, chemical stability, and tunable nanochannel structures suitable for organic solvent

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: Available online 9 August 2025 0376-7388/ 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. This is an open access article under the CC BY license ( http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ). nanofiltration [11–14]. BNNS, is a typical graphene-like 2D material, known as its high organic and chemical stability [15]. The hydrophobic nature and lack of aqueous functional groups have traditionally made it challenging to assemble them into membranes or provide strong linking point with other materials [16]. This issue has been well addressed with functionalized BNNS, resulting in membranes with significantly high permeance [14,16]. However, mechanical strength is a significant concern for all 2D nanomaterial-based membranes including BNNS membranes, due to the weak interlayer van der Waals force within their 2D laminated channels [17]. The interlayer swelling phenomena is another issue for rich functional groups containing 2D material mem­branes, significantly compromising their mechanical strength and cor­responding stability, especially during long-term use under harsh conditions [17]. While stacking larger-scale 2D nanosheets can improve mechanical strength, these membranes still hardly harness the tensile strength requirements, especially under higher applied pressure [18,19]. The multi-dimensional TFC membranes demonstrated their effec­tiveness for overcoming challenges and balancing the performance [16,20,21].

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: ure [18,19]. The multi-dimensional TFC membranes demonstrated their effec­tiveness for overcoming challenges and balancing the performance [16,20,21]. Therefore, the functionalized BNNS based polymer composite membranes could be a potential candidature to provide robust mix-dimensional molecular channels with high permeance. Herein, a robust high-flux hybrid ANF/BNNS (ABN) TFC membrane was successfully developed. The process involved fully exchanging the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) in the ANF colloid dispersion with DI water, leading to the deformation of ANF with enlarged diameters of about 37 nm. The water deformed ANFs were then integrated into the BNNS in­terlayers to enhance the mechanical strength of the hybrid membranes. This integration leveraged the amide groups of ANFs to form strong intermolecular hydrogen bonds with the BNNS. Such hydrogen bonding interactions are well-documented in polymer systems, as they enhance mechanical properties by providing stronger and more stable intermo­lecular interactions compared to van der Waals or electrostatic forces. For instance, hydrogen bonds have been shown to improve membrane stability and gas selectivity in polyimide systems [22,23].

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: or electrostatic forces. For instance, hydrogen bonds have been shown to improve membrane stability and gas selectivity in polyimide systems [22,23]. Additionally, the bonding energy of hydrogen bonds, being significantly higher than van der Waals forces, contributed to the enhanced tensile strength observed in the ABN membranes, similar to the improved mechanical performance observed in other polymeric systems with tailored hydrogen bonding networks [24]. The resulting ABN membrane exhibited a high ethanol permeance of about 104 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 and high selectivity (>95 %) towards Evans blue (EB) molecules. Further­more, the membrane demonstrated superior mechanical strength of about 30 MPa, allowing for reversible selective channel deformation under applied pressures up to 10 bar. The rejection window of the membrane could be modulated from ~400 to ~900 Da by tuning the loading mass and thickness, enabling selective separation of solutes with different molecular weight (MW) such as EB, Methyl Orange (MO) and Amaranth (AM) in ethanol. Additionally, the ABN membranes showed high chemical stability in a wide range of organic solvents and acid solutions, as well as long-term anti-fouling ability. These results indicate the high potential of ABN membranes for practical applications in mo­lecular separation. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Chemicals Kevlar 69 fibers was bought from DuPont Pty. Ltd.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: mbranes for practical applications in mo­lecular separation. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Chemicals Kevlar 69 fibers was bought from DuPont Pty. Ltd. Hexagonal boron nitride powder (h-BN) was bought from Momentive Performance Ma­terials Inc. Potassium hydroxide (KOH, ACS reagent ≥85 %), sodium alginate (12,000–40000 Da), DMSO (ACS reagent, 99.7 %), hydrochlo­ric acid (HCl, ACS reagent 37 %), nanoporous anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) substrate, urea (ACS Reagent, 99.0–100.5 %) and all dyes with different molecular weights were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Pty. Ltd. Nylon filters (100 nm pore size, 47 mm) were purchased from Hawach Scientific CO., Ltd.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: 2.2. Preparation of BNNS dispersion The h-BN nanosheet dispersion was prepared using a modified ball milling method [25]. A mixture of 10.5 g of bulk h-BN and urea in a 1:20 mass ratio, along with 10 stainless steel balls (10 mm in diameter), was placed in a stainless steel milling jar. The mixture was milled for 5 h at 400 rpm using a Fritsch P7 ball mill. After milling, the resulting powders were transferred and dissolved in DI water. The h-BN dispersion was obtained by dialyzing the mixture in deionized water for one week using a membrane with a 3500 kDa cutoff to remove the residual urea. The BNNS dispersion (~1.75 mg mL-1) was obtained by centrifuging at 3000 rpm for 10 min. 2.3. Preparation of ANF-water dispersion and ANF/BNNS dispersion ANF colloidal dispersion was prepared by mixing 2.0 g of Kevlar 69 fibers and 2.0 g of KOH in 100 mL of DMSO, followed by stirring at room temperature for one week to obtain a viscous and homogeneous dark red ANF-DMSO dispersion. This dispersion was then diluted to 2 mg mL-1 with DMSO and excess KOH. Using vacuum filtration with continuous manual stirring to prevent film formation, the DMSO and residual KOH were fully exchanged with approximately 2 L of deionized water. Finally, a 1 L deformed ANF DI water dispersion was achieved with a concentration of about 0.7 mg mL-1. Then, combine 20 mL of BNNS dispersion with 5 mL of ANF-water dispersion, maintaining a mass ratio of 1:10.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: with a concentration of about 0.7 mg mL-1. Then, combine 20 mL of BNNS dispersion with 5 mL of ANF-water dispersion, maintaining a mass ratio of 1:10. Subsequently, dilute the mixture to a total volume of 200 mL (~0.2 mg mL-1) with deionized water to obtain the ANF/BNNS dispersion. 2.4. Fabrication of OSN membranes The OSN membranes were prepared by vacuum filtration of the asprepared dispersions onto a nylon support membrane (47 mm in diameter, 0.1 μm pore size). The resulting membranes were then dried in an oven at 60 ◦C overnight. The thickness of the membranes was controlled by varying the amount of dispersion deposited. The prepared ABN membranes were designated as ABN-X, where X indicates the thickness of the membranes. 2.5. Characterization Morphological and structural features of few-layer BNNS were characterized using atomic force microscopy (AFM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (see Section 3.1 for details). The morphol­ogies of the membranes and the cross-sectional area were obtained using two scanning electron microscopes (SEM), the Supra 55 V (Zeiss) and the 7800 F (JEOL). To reduce charging effects, all membranes were coated with a 5 nm carbon layer using a high vacuum coating instrument (ACE600, Leica). Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectrometry (EDS) data was also collected from the Supra 55 V. TEM images were taken from a transmission electron microscope (2100 F, JEOL).

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: ve X-ray Spectrometry (EDS) data was also collected from the Supra 55 V. TEM images were taken from a transmission electron microscope (2100 F, JEOL). AFM (Multimode 8, Bruker) was used to characterize the morphology and surface roughness of the as-prepared ANF, BNNS, and ABN membranes. Each dispersion (~1 mg L-1) was dropped onto a silicon wafer mounted on a holder using double-sided tape, followed by solvent evaporation. Membrane samples were cut into ~1 cm * 1 cm pieces and fixed onto magnetic substrates. All images were acquired in tapping mode in air with 512 lines per scan at 1 Hz and processed using NanoScope software. Mem­brane zeta potential data was derived from the Surpass 3 (Anton, Paar). UV–Vis spectroscopy (UV Vis 300, Cary) was used to measure dye concentrations in the feed, retentate, and permeate solutions. Reflec­tance infrared spectroscopy spectra (Invenio-R, Bruker) of the prepared membranes were acquired with a spectrometer. Contact angle mea­surements were conducted using an optical tensiometer (Theta flow, Attention).

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: 2.6. Organic solvent nanofiltration performance The performance of organic solvent nanofiltration was assessed using a stainless-steel dead-end membrane module (Stirred Cells, STERLI­TECH) with an effective membrane area of 7.07 cm2 (a circular area with a diameter of 3 cm). During a typical nanofiltration test as previ­ously reported [14,26], the module was operated at certain bars, regu­lated by a nitrogen cylinder, with constant stirring at 500 rpm to minimize concentration polarization. Each membrane was tested with 40 mL of feed solution containing approximately 10 mg L-1 dye. Prior to each nanofiltration experiment, the membranes were preconditioned by filtering three sequential 30 mL aliquots from the 50 mL dye solution feed. This step served to minimize the influence of initial adsorption or entrapment effects, ensuring that the reported rejection values reflect steady-state separation performance. The concentrations of the feed, permeate, and retentate solutions were measured using a UV–Vis spec­trophotometer after collecting 20 mL of solvent through the membranes. Calibration curves were established for MO, AM, and EB in ethanol within a concentration range of 0.5–2.5 mg L-1. The absorbance at each dye’s maximum absorption wavelength was recorded, and linear regression analysis yielded correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.9919, 0.9901, and 0.9967, respectively (Fig. S1). These curves were used to calculate dye concentrations and rejection values.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: coefficients (R2) of 0.9919, 0.9901, and 0.9967, respectively (Fig. S1). These curves were used to calculate dye concentrations and rejection values. The solvent flux (Flux, L⋅m-2 h-1) was calculated from equation (1): Flux =VA * t(1) where V (L) is the volume of the permeating solvents, A (m2) is the effective membrane working area (7.07 * 10-4 m2), t (h) is the permeation time. The membrane permeance (P, L⋅m-2 h-1 bar-1) was calculated from equation (2): P =FDriving Force(2) Where the driving force is the transmembrane pressure difference. The rejection (R) was calculated using equation (3): R(%) =(1 -CpCf )* 100%(3) where CP and Cf represent the concentrations of the permeate and feed. 2.7. Swelling degree The stability of the as-prepared ABN-1.71 membranes in pure sol­vents, including acetone, methanol, ethanol, acetonitrile, 2-butanone, IPA, DMSO, and DMF, was evaluated by comparing their dry weight (m1) and wet weight (m2). The dry weight was obtained by placing the ABN samples in an oven at 60 ◦C overnight. The samples were then immersed in the various pure solvents at 25 ◦C for 7 days. The wet weight was recorded by weighing the samples immediately after the removal of the swollen membrane from the liquid and gently blot it with the Kimwipes disposable wipers to remove excess surface water without pressing or deforming the membrane. The swelling degree was calculated as equation (4): Swelling degree = m2 -m1m1* 100%(4) 2.8.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: surface water without pressing or deforming the membrane. The swelling degree was calculated as equation (4): Swelling degree = m2 -m1m1* 100%(4) 2.8. Performance and pH stability tests For pH testing, following the same OSN procedure described in section 2.6, the EB ethanol solutions (~10 mg L-1) were filtered through the as-prepared membranes, with the pH adjusted between 4 and 10 by adding 0.01 M HCl and KOH aqueous solutions. To evaluate performance consistency under continuous operation, the membranes were tested with EB ethanol solutions (~10 mg L-1) for 25 h at a con­stant pressure of 1 bar. 2.9. Antifouling performance A sodium alginate solution (12,000–40,000 Da) with a concentration of 1 g L-1 was used as the feed solution to evaluate the anti-fouling and flux recovery properties of the ABN membrane. Initially, the pure water was filtered through the ABN membrane for 5 h. Then, 1 L of sodium alginate solution was filtered through the ABN membrane to simulate an anti-fouling test. The membranes were removed from the fouling solu­tion and rinsed with DI water three times. Subsequently, a DI water nanofiltration test was conducted three times to calculate the flux re­covery (FR). The calculation was using as equation (5): FR (%) = F1F2 * 100%(5) Where F1 refers to the pure water flux for ABN membranes after the simulated antifouling test and F2 is pure water flux of the ABN mem­branes before the simulated antifouling test. 3. Results and discussion 3.1.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: r the simulated antifouling test and F2 is pure water flux of the ABN mem­branes before the simulated antifouling test. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Characterization of BNNS and water deformed ANFs As illustrated in Fig. S2, the ABN membranes were fabricated by depositing water-deformed ANFs and ball-milled BNNS mixture dispersion onto a commercial nylon substrate (Fig. S3) using a vacuum filtration procedure. The disordered stacking of few-layer BNNS in­dicates the successful exfoliation of bulk h-BN after the revised ball milling process (Fig. 1a). The morphology of ball-milled few-layer BNNS reveals that the lateral size can even reach the microscale (Fig. 1b). The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the edge of a single ball-milled few-layer BNNS shows the presence of 4–6 layers (Fig. 1c). The characteristic spacing of 2.17 Å corresponds to the (10-10) (Armchair edge) confirming the crystallinity of h-BN (Fig. 1d) [27,28]. The thickness of the ball-milled few-layer BNNS was further character­ized using atomic force microscope (AFM), revealing an average thick­ness of approximately 1.2 nm (Fig. 1e). This AFM thickness result is consistent with the TEM crystalline result, considering the d-spacing of h-BN is about 0.33 nm [29,30]. This outcome is closely related to the modified ball milling procedure, in which a higher urea-to-h-BN mass ratio and reduced milling duration were employed.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: tcome is closely related to the modified ball milling procedure, in which a higher urea-to-h-BN mass ratio and reduced milling duration were employed. Despite a slight in­crease in thickness (to 4–6 layers), the BNNS exhibited significantly larger lateral size and improved aqueous dispersibility, which facilitated uniform blending with ANFs and promoted membrane formation with enhanced interfacial compatibility and mechanical integrity. According to the previous reports, introducing DI water into the DMSO/KOH ANF dispersion would increase the deprotonation degree and lead to the disintegration of ANFs into a smaller diameter distribution of approxi­mately 10 nm [9,31]. Additionally, in our previous work, we success­fully demonstrated that varying the concentration of KOH and utilizing different organic solvents could effectively enlarge the size of ANFs [10]. Building on this, we explored an alternative approach to control the deprotonation degree of ANFs by completely replacing the solvents with DI water and omitting KOH. This investigation aimed to examine the effects of deprotonation degree on the size and corresponding diameter deformation of ANFs. As a result, the morphology of water-deformed ANFs is depicted in Fig. 1f–h. Following complete solvent exchange, the water deformed ANFs maintained a rigid fibrous shape, with a slightly increased average diameter of approximately 37 nm (Fig. 1i).

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: 3.2. Morphological characterization of ABN membranes A typical ABN TFC membrane was assembled on a commercial nylon substrate with a circular active area, deposited from the as-prepared ABN dispersion (Fig. 2a). The pristine ANF membrane, BNNS mem­brane, and ABN membrane all demonstrated a uniform surface morphology (Fig. 2b and S4). Compared to the pristine BNNS mem­brane, the ABN membrane exhibited a similar BNNS stacking morphology, with observable slits indicating the stacking status of BNNS. The ABN membranes exhibited a stratified internal architecture, in which ANFs appeared to interconnect BNNS domains [32], contrib­uting to the overall layered morphology. EDS mappings reveal the dis­tribution areas of elements B, N, and O, indicating the composite structure of BNNS and ANFs (Fig. 2c). Notably, the ABN membrane maintains a uniform structure and retains its layered configuration (Fig. 2d and S5). Compared to our previous work, the cross-section of ABN membranes exhibits a more compact structure [14]. Furthermore, by adjusting the loading mass of the as-prepared ABN dispersion, the thickness of ABN membranes was tailored from approximately 650 nm to 2 μm (Fig. 2e). The presence of C, N, O, and B elements in the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum for a typical ABN membrane, as indicated by Fig. S6a, confirms the similar chemical composition shown in the EDS results. As shown in Fig.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: ey spectrum for a typical ABN membrane, as indicated by Fig. S6a, confirms the similar chemical composition shown in the EDS results. As shown in Fig. 2f, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of N 1s is deconvoluted into C–NH–C bonds (399.3 eV) and N–B bonds (397.9 eV) [33,34]. Fig. S6c presents the O 1s spectrum, which can be deconvoluted into two peaks with binding energies at 531.34 eV and 532.37 eV, corresponding to O––C–C and O––C–N bonds, respectively [35]. Additionally, Fig. S6d shows the XPS spectrum of C 1s, which can be deconvoluted into three peaks representing C––C/C–C in aromatic rings (284.6 eV), C–N groups (286.4 eV), and C––O groups (288.8 eV) [36]. The functional groups were further verified by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2g, the absorption peak of ABN membranes was observed at approximately 1639 cm-1, which was assigned to the C––O stretching vibration from amide groups [9]. Meanwhile, the peak at 1346 cm-1 corresponds to the B–N–B bond from BNNS [14]. Compared to pristine BNNS membranes, the peak of the B–N–B bond shifts from 1373 cm-1 to 1353 cm-1, indicating the influence of hydrogen bonding involving the nitrogen atom in BN as a hydrogen bond acceptor. Similarly, peak shifts in the N–H bond as a hydrogen bond donor were also observed among the membranes, with the peak slightly shifting from 3295 cm-1 (BNNS and ANF) to 3292 cm-1 (ABN).

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: ond as a hydrogen bond donor were also observed among the membranes, with the peak slightly shifting from 3295 cm-1 (BNNS and ANF) to 3292 cm-1 (ABN). In addition to morphological evidence, spectroscopic characterizations also support the interaction between ANFs and BNNS. Specifically, the slight red shift of the N–H stretching vibration peak from 3295 cm-1 (ANF) to 3292 cm-1 (ABN) in FTIR spectra suggests the involvement of N–H groups in hydrogen bonding with BNNS. While these findings do not directly visualize covalent bonding, they provide strong evidence of interfacial cross-linking through non-covalent interactions (i.e., hydrogen bonding), which en­hances the structural integrity of the composite membrane. The tensile strength tests were conducted to assess changes in me­chanical strength of ABN TFC membranes. Introducing 10 wt% ANFs by mass into BNNS significantly increased the tensile strength of TFC membranes from 27.4 to 30.5 MPa, despite a slight decrease compared

content:

(c) TEM image of stacked BNNS; inset: edge view of a few-layer sheet (scale bar: 5 nm). (d) Dark-field HRTEM image of a few-layer BNNS. (e) AFM image of a few-layer BNNS and its height profile. (f, g) SEM In-lens images of water-based ANFs on an AAO substrate at different magnifications. (h) 3D AFM image of water-based ANFs and the corresponding lateral height profile. citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: Fig. 3. (a–c) Water contact angles of BNNS, ANF, and ABN membranes. (d–f) 3D AFM images of corresponding surfaces. Y. Ma et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124540 to pure ANF membranes (Fig. 2i). Fig. 2j presents the zeta potentials of the ABN, pristine ANF, and BNNS membranes over a pH range from 4 to 10. The BNNS membrane consistently showed the highest surface charge across almost all pH levels. When ANFs were incorporated into BNNS to fabricate the ABN membranes, the surface charge slightly decreased. This indicates that the BNNS and ANFs were uniformly integrated and some BNNS were linked by amide groups from ANFs through hydrogen bonding. When the pH was at 7, the absolute value of the zeta potential of these membranes followed the order: BNNS (17.75) > ABN (14.98) >ANF (13.31). The water contact angles were measured to assess the hydrophilicity of the membranes. All membranes exhibited hydrophilic characteristics, with contact angles of 52.03◦, 73.58◦, and 81.10◦for ANF, BNNS, and ABN membranes, respectively (Fig. 3a–c). According to the Wenzel model, increased surface roughness enhances the contact area between the membrane surface and the water droplets, facilitating the spreading of the droplets [37]. Thus, the ANF membrane exhibited the highest hydrophilicity, which can be attributed to its higher surface roughness.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: spreading of the droplets [37]. Thus, the ANF membrane exhibited the highest hydrophilicity, which can be attributed to its higher surface roughness. In addition to roughness, the quantity and nature of hydro­philic functional groups on the membrane surface significantly influence hydrophilicity [38]. The ABN membrane showed lower hydrophilicity compared to the BNNS membrane, which can be attributed to its rela­tively lower surface zeta potential, as smaller zeta potential values typically correlate with decreased hydrophilicity. This is consistent with previous reports, where higher zeta potential values were associated with more hydrophilic surfaces [39,40]. The membrane surface rough­ness was measured over an area of 225 μm2, divided into 9 equal areas, and calculated by the average Ra (Fig. S7). The BNNS membrane exhibited a smoother surface, whereas the ANF membrane displayed the highest surface roughness. Upon introducing ANF to BNNS, there was a slight increase in membrane surface roughness, attributing to the link­ages between BNNS and ANFs (Fig. 3d–f). 3.3. Organic nanofiltration performance of ABN membranes The organic nanofiltration performance of the membranes was evaluated using anionic dyes, including MO (MW, 327 Da), AM (MW, 604 Da), and EB (MW, 960 Da), to minimize the impact of adsorption between dye molecules and the negatively charged membrane surfaces.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: 27 Da), AM (MW, 604 Da), and EB (MW, 960 Da), to minimize the impact of adsorption between dye molecules and the negatively charged membrane surfaces. These charged organic molecules were selected to evaluate the molec­ular rejection characteristics of the membranes. It is important to note that their observed rejection is governed not only by size exclusion but also by electrostatic interactions between the dye molecules and the negatively charged membrane surface [41]. Thus, the rejection effi­ciency of these dyes provides insights into the membrane’s performance in both size exclusion and charge-related filtration processes. Fig. 4presents a schematic illustration of the organic solvent nanofiltration mechanism. The incorporation of ANFs enhanced the mechanical strength of ABN membranes and refined the selective pores/channels, enabling high permeance while maintaining excellent rejection of various dye molecules. The higher concentration observed in the retentate solution and the nearly transparent color of the permeate confirm the successful sepa­ration of EB molecules during the OSN test using the ABN-1.17 mem­brane (Fig. 5a). Fig. 5b illustrates the separation efficiency of ABN membranes with varying thicknesses for EB molecules. Increasing the membrane thickness from 650 nm to 1.17 μm resulted in a rapid increase in EB rejection from 82 % to 96 %, further reaching over 99 % for thicknesses greater than 1.17 μm.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: rom 650 nm to 1.17 μm resulted in a rapid increase in EB rejection from 82 % to 96 %, further reaching over 99 % for thicknesses greater than 1.17 μm. Simultaneously, the permeance of ABN membranes decreased from 159 ± 16 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 to 16 ± 2 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 when thickness increased from 650 nm to 2.02 μm. The results suggest that a loading mass of 0.23 mg cm-2 and a membrane thickness of 1.17 μm achieved the optimal balance between ethanol permeance of about 104 ± 14 L m-2 h-1 bar-1 and selectivity for EB molecules of over 95 % in the nanofiltration range (200–1000 Da), considering EB as one of the largest molecules of 960 Da. Smaller-sized dyes were also used to assess the OSN performance of ABN membranes (Fig. 5c). For the AM molecules, the rejection rates of the ABN mem­branes with thicknesses of 650 nm, 1.17 μm, 1.59 μm, 1.71 μm, and 2.02 μm were 48 %, 68 %, 72 %, 91 %, and 99 %, respectively. Similarly, the rejection rates for MO molecules followed the same trend, rising from 19 % (650 nm) to 37 % (1.17 μm), 39 % (1.59 μm), 58 % (1.71 μm), and 86 % (2.02 μm). The observed rejection of solutes with varying molec­ular weights was analyzed to examine the membrane size-exclusion behaviors. As shown in Fig. 5d, the ABN membrane exhibited increased rejection of larger solutes with increasing membrane thick­ness.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: e size-exclusion behaviors. As shown in Fig. 5d, the ABN membrane exhibited increased rejection of larger solutes with increasing membrane thick­ness. For example, solutes such as AM and EB were more effectively retained by thicker membranes, while thinner membranes achieved ≥90 % rejection only for higher-molecular-weight solutes. These trends indicate tunable molecular separation performance via thickness con­trol, though an exact molecular weight cut-off value cannot be assigned due to electrostatic effects and solute aggregation in ethanol. The results highlighted the potential of ABN membranes for precise molecular sieving, even for smaller solutes such as salt/dye separations. The applied loading pressure is crucial for exploring the optimal balance of membrane performance. This involves meeting the high mechanical strength requirements essential for the selective pores or channels to endure maximum loading pressure, thereby ensuring high separation efficiency. Pure 2D nanomaterial-based OSN nanofiltration membranes often face challenges such as irreversible channel breakages, particularly under higher loading pressures [18]. Therefore, the ABN membranes were tested by using pure ethanol at pressures up to 10 bar, considering the typical operating range of 1–10 bar in OSN, to assess the

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: Y. Ma et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124540 performance of their selective pores/channels. According to the Hagen-Poiseuille equation, the linear relationship between loading pressure and solvent flux corresponds to viscous flow within the selec­tive channels [42]. The nearly coinciding trends between forward and backward testing, along with the linear relationship dependent on applied pressures, indicate that the selective pores/channels can reversibly deform, underscoring that the ABN-1.17 membrane can withstand external pressures up to 10 bar (Fig. 6a). The details are listed in Table S1. These findings suggest the robustness of stacked pores or channels, where ANFs interact strongly with BNNS, contributing to enhanced tensile strength. Additionally, the results indicate the poten­tial to surpass previous trade-offs. Therefore, the EB ethanol solution was used as the solute to assess the ABN-1.17 membranes, aiming to optimize the balance between flux and selectivity. Fig. 6b demonstrates that the ABN-1.17 membrane achieves a balance between ethanol flux and rejection for EB molecules under varying pressures. At 4 bar, it achieves an ethanol flux of approximately 164 L m-2 h-1 with a rejec­tion of 92.34 %. The rejection slightly decreases to 89.66 % at 6 bar and 86.35 % at 8 bar, accompanied by fluxes of 188 L m-2 h-1 and 201 L m-2 h-1, respectively.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: %. The rejection slightly decreases to 89.66 % at 6 bar and 86.35 % at 8 bar, accompanied by fluxes of 188 L m-2 h-1 and 201 L m-2 h-1, respectively. Finally, the membrane reaches its highest ethanol flux of 414 L m-2 h-1 with a rejection of 67.8 %, highlighting the po­tential to achieve optimal separation efficiency. It was observed that as the applied pressure increased, both selectivity and permeance decreased. This phenomenon can be attributed to the deformation of certain pores under high pressure, resulting in an uneven arrangement of pore channels. Such unevenness can create non-selective permeation pathways, enabling solute molecules to bypass the size-exclusion mechanism and thereby reducing the rejection [43]. Concurrently, the compaction effect under high pressure decreases the effective pore size and porosity [44]. This compaction further reduces the membrane permeability, even as the presence of non-selective pathways facilitates solute permeation. As the applied pressure increased, both selectivity and permeance decreased due to reversible pore deformation and compaction, which restricts overall molecular transport. This finding underscores the necessity of maintaining well-defined channel struc­tures to ensure selective transport, a principle also exemplified in COF membranes where lithium-ion selectivity relies heavily on sub-nanometer charged pores and ion–framework interactions [45,46].

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: also exemplified in COF membranes where lithium-ion selectivity relies heavily on sub-nanometer charged pores and ion–framework interactions [45,46]. Moreover, to explore practical applications and evaluate the potential for precise molecular separation, an ABN-1.17 membrane was applied for separating a mixture of dyes, AM and EB, with a mass ratio of approximately 1:1 in ethanol solution. The UV absorption spectra and the inset indicate that AM molecules selectively permeate through a typical ABN-1.17 membrane, while EB molecules were predominantly retained, as evidenced by the absence of a blue hue in the permeate and diminished absorbance in the 600–650 nm region (Fig. 6c).The results further demonstrate the potential for achieving precise sieving with the series of ABN membranes simply varying in thickness. Additionally, the OSN performance of the ABN membranes with 2D nanomaterial-based and polymeric-based membranes was comprehensively compared with the recent literature (Table S2). While some reported OSN membranes (Fig. 6d) exhibit higher flux or rejection, the ABN membranes developed here offer a favorable balance between separation performance and practical scalability. Compared with conventional polymeric mem­branes, which often suffer from limited permeance in organic solvents due to dense structures and low porosity, the ABN membranes achieve significantly higher ethanol permeance while maintaining effective molecular rejection.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: o dense structures and low porosity, the ABN membranes achieve significantly higher ethanol permeance while maintaining effective molecular rejection. In contrast to fragile 2D material-based membranes (e.g., GO or MoS2 nanosheets), which typically require supporting sub­strates or display brittleness under mechanical stress, the ABN mem­branes demonstrate excellent flexibility, structural integrity, and selfstanding capability. These features position ABN membranes as

content: Fig. 5. Organic solvent nanofiltration performance. (a) UV spectra of retentate, feed and permeate ethanol solutions across a typical ABN-1.17 membrane. The inset shows the color among three solutions, which indicates the concentration of EB. (b) Ethanol permeance and EB (Evans blue) rejection for as-prepared ABN mem­branes. (c) Separation performance of ABN membranes under various dye (MO, AM and EB) ethanol solutions. (d) Observed rejection (%) of dye solutes with molecular weights of 327, 604, and 960 Da for membranes of varying thicknesses. The rejection window is shown to shift with increasing membrane thickness, indicating tunable molecular sieving behavior. Error bars represent the standard deviation from at least three independent samples. (For interpretation of the ref­erences to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.) Y. Ma et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124540

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: Fig. 7. (a) Relation between inverse viscosity and permeance under various organic solvents across a typical ABN-1.71 membrane. (b) Long term stability of a typical ABN-1.71 membrane. (c) pH stability of a typical ABN-1.71 membrane. (d) Antifouling performance of a typical ABN-1.71 membrane. Y. Ma et al. Journal of Membrane Science 735 (2025) 124540 promising candidates for scalable fabrication, module integration, and practical industrial applications. Other common organic solvents, both polar and nonpolar, were used to test the ABN membranes to investigate the relationship between permeance and these solvents. The details of the organic solvents are listed in Table S3. As solvent viscosity increased, the permeance of ABN1.71 membranes decreased (Fig. 7a). When acetone was used, the ABN membranes achieved the highest permeance, whereas in hexane, despite its lower viscosity, the membranes exhibited the lowest permeance among the tested solvents (Table S4). This may be attributed to hexane’s nonpolar nature and solvent-polymer interactions [47]. Interestingly, the flux for aprotic solvents is generally higher than for protic solvents with similar viscosity. The strong interactions between organic solvent molecules and the functional groups on the surface of the selective pores/channels may hinder solvent transport. The stability of the OSN membrane is another crucial factor for practical applications.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: of the selective pores/channels may hinder solvent transport. The stability of the OSN membrane is another crucial factor for practical applications. Eight common organic solvents (Table 1) were selected to evaluate the swelling degrees of ABN membranes. The swelling degrees in methanol, ethanol, and IPA were 53 %, 89 %, and 36 %, respectively, while in DMF, acetonitrile, and DMSO, the values increased to 220 %, 182 %, and 106 %, respectively (Table 1). This is likely because the weaker interactions between aprotic solvent mole­cules and the membrane allow the solvent molecules to penetrate the nanofiber matrix more easily, disrupting its structure and leading to higher swelling. The stability of ABN membranes was evaluated over a 24 h period using an EB ethanol solution under 1 bar. As depicted in Fig. 7b, the ethanol permeance through an ABN-1.71 membrane remained stable at approximately 30 L m-2 h-1 bar-1, with a high steady-state rejection of about 95 % throughout the 24 h testing period. The OSN performance of ABN membranes was further tested under extreme pH conditions using HCl and KOH aqueous solutions (~0.01 M) by adjusting the pH from 4 to 10 in an EB ethanol solution. As shown in Fig. 7c, the ABN membranes maintained an average flux of around 22 L m-2 h-1 across all pH ranges. However, the rejection decreased below 90 % as the pH increased greater than 7. The phenomenon can be attributed to the deprotonation of amide groups under alkaline conditions.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: reased below 90 % as the pH increased greater than 7. The phenomenon can be attributed to the deprotonation of amide groups under alkaline conditions. Furthermore, the deproto­nation of these functional groups can disrupt interactions such as hydrogen bonding or electrostatic forces within the selective pores/ channels, leading to lower rejections observed at higher pH values. Additionally, anti-fouling properties (Fig. 7d) were assessed using a sodium alginate aqueous solution (12,000–40,000 Da) with a concen­tration of 1 g L-1 to simulate large-sized contaminants permeating through a typical ABN-1.71 membrane. Following a 5-day simulation test, the water flux of the ABN-1.71 membrane decreased from 57 to 42 L m-2 h-1, with a total water flux recovery ratio of 73 %. 4. Conclusions In summary, we have developed ABN thin-film composite mem­branes via a simple, scalable co-deposition strategy. The resulting membranes exhibit tunable molecular rejection performance across the solute molecular weight range of ~327–960 Da, depending on mem­brane thickness, demonstrating controlled separation behavior in ethanol. These characteristics highlight the potential of ABN membranes for OSN, particularly for molecular sieving in ethanolic media. While several OSN membranes in the literature exhibit higher permeance or sharper selectivity, the ABN membranes represent a balanced trade-off between performance, mechanical durability, and fabrication simplicity.

citation.title: ANF/BN thin-film composite membranes for efficient organic solvent nanofiltration

content: eance or sharper selectivity, the ABN membranes represent a balanced trade-off between performance, mechanical durability, and fabrication simplicity. In contrast to conventional polymeric membranes, they offer enhanced solvent permeance due to their nanoscale interconnectivity; compared to membranes derived from 2D materials, they exhibit supe­rior flexibility, selfstanding integrity, and potential for mechanical resilience, which is the key attributes for process integration and oper­ational stability. These advantages arise from the interpenetrated nanofiber-nanosheet network, which imparts both transport efficiency and structural reinforcement. Taken together, the ABN membranes provide a promising platform for practical OSN applications, where scalability, robustness, and separation performance must be simulta­neously addressed.
